text: The surgeon performs moderate lysis of adhesions, revealing the abdominal wall defect in the right abdomen.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal plane starting on the left side of the midline and extends it across the midline. On the right side, the posterior rectus sheath is incised to enter the retrorectus plane, which is then extended laterally. The lateral posterior rectus sheath is incised, allowing re-entry into the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal plane near the abdominal wall defect, carefully separating the peritoneum to reveal the intact external oblique muscle.
The surgeon completes the release of the peritoneum from the area of weakness in the abdominal wall, fully separating it from the underlying structures.

The surgeon dissects over the transplanted kidney to create lateral overlap for mesh placement. After achieving the necessary overlap, the fascial defect is closed with a running number one Stratafix barbed suture.
The surgeon inserts an 18 by 5 centimeters soft barred mesh over the abdominal wall defect to reinforce the primary repair, securing it with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl sutures.
A 5mm trocar is inserted into the right upper quadrant, facilitating the introduction of a 19 French Blake drain into the extraperitoneal space next to the mesh.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a running two O V-lock barbed suture, completing the repair in the area of the abdominal wall defect.
The surgeon opens the surgical drain to the air, facilitating the evacuation of CO2 from the extraperitoneal space.
